title: Vegetarian full English
subtitle: Fried eggs, portobello mushrooms, roasted tomatoes, and baked beans on toast.
excerpt: Two fried eggs, portobello mushrooms and cherry tomatoes roasted in the oven, baked beans heated through, and thick toast. Everything timed to be ready together. No sausages needed.
sourceNotes: The full English breakfast as a defined meal emerged in Victorian Britain. The vegetarian version became a standard restaurant and café offering as vegetarianism grew in Britain from the 1980s onwards, following the Animal Liberation movement and the rise of vegetarian cookbooks. The format removes the meat elements and substitutes mushrooms and additional eggs for their savoury weight.

content.text: The vegetarian full English works as a meal because the portobello mushrooms carry the savoury weight that sausages and bacon usually provide. Roasting them in the oven concentrates their flavour and gives them a slightly firmer texture than pan-frying.
The timing is the main skill here. Mushrooms and tomatoes go in the oven first. Beans go on ten minutes later. Eggs go in the pan for the last four minutes. Everything finishes at once.
A baking tray for the oven elements, a small frying pan for the eggs, and a small saucepan for the beans.

Heat the oven to 200 °C fan. Put the portobello mushrooms gill-side up and the cherry tomatoes on a baking tray. Drizzle with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of salt. Roast for 15 to 18 minutes until the mushrooms are tender and the tomatoes are softened and beginning to burst.
Heat the beans and make the toast
After 8 minutes of oven time, heat the baked beans in a small saucepan over a low-medium heat, stirring occasionally. Put the bread in the toaster when the oven has about 5 minutes left.

When the mushrooms and tomatoes have about 4 minutes left, melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small frying pan over a medium-low heat. Crack in {{eggs}} of eggs and fry for 3 to 4 minutes until the whites are set and the yolks remain runny. Season with {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Serve everything immediately on warm plates.

items.fix: Roast at 200 °C fan with the mushrooms gill-side up so liquid evaporates rather than pools. Space them apart on the tray.
items.cause: Roasted at too low a temperature or crowded on the tray.

items.fix: Medium-low heat and a lid for the last minute of cooking keeps the whites setting while the yolk stays runny.
items.cause: Heat too high or cooked too long.
items.symptom: Egg whites are set but yolks are hard

items.fix: The oven elements take the longest. Start the beans at 8 minutes in; start the eggs at 14 minutes in; bread into the toaster at 14 minutes in. Adjust based on your oven.
Add a splash of water and stir over low heat.
items.cause: Reduced down too much in the pan.

content.text: The full English is a nineteenth-century plate, built around the idea that breakfast should be filling enough to last a working day. The vegetarian version keeps the same idea without the meat. It works because eggs, toast, beans, and roasted vegetables already give the protein and the staying power the meal is built around. Portobello mushrooms became the usual meat swap because their flavour deepens with heat in a way raw mushrooms do not.
